theory.intro: Pythagoras' theorem links the three sides of a right-angled triangle. Stating it correctly — in symbols and in words — is what every later Pythagoras calculation relies on.
facts: **Pythagoras' theorem:** in a right-angled triangle, $a^2+b^2=h^2$, where $h$ is the hypotenuse and $a$ and $b$ are the two shorter sides.
In words: the **sum of the squares of the two shorter sides equals the square of the hypotenuse**.
The hypotenuse term sits **on its own** on one side of the equation, because it is the side opposite the right angle.
The letters are only labels. If the hypotenuse is called $r$, the theorem is $p^2+q^2=r^2$; the hypotenuse is still squared by itself.
Read the diagram, not the alphabet: whichever side is opposite the right angle is the one squared on its own.

foundation.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (4,0);\coordinate (C) at (0,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$b$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$a$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above right] {$c$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Write Pythagoras' theorem for this triangle.

[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (4,0);\coordinate (B) at (0,0);\coordinate (C) at (4,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$x$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,right] {$y$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above left] {$z$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Write Pythagoras' theorem for this triangle.

[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (4,0);\coordinate (C) at (0,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$y$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$w$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above right] {$x$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The hypotenuse is $x$. Write Pythagoras' theorem for this triangle.
Complete Pythagoras' theorem in words: the sum of the squares of the two shorter sides equals ___.

[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (0,3);\coordinate (B) at (0,0);\coordinate (C) at (4,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,left] {$s$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,above] {$t$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,below right] {$u$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Write Pythagoras' theorem for this triangle.

development.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (J) at (1,1.732);\coordinate (L) at (-2,0);\coordinate (K) at (2,0);\draw (J)--(L) node[midway,above left] {$p$};\draw (J)--(K) node[midway,above right] {$q$};\draw (L)--(K) node[midway,below] {$r$};\coordinate (va) at ($(J)!0.3cm!(L)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(J)!0.3cm!(K)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(J)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The right angle is at the top vertex. Write Pythagoras' theorem for this triangle.
development.solution_text:
The hypotenuse is $r$, opposite the right angle, so
$p^2+q^2=r^2$

development.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (4,0);\coordinate (C) at (0,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$b$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$a$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above right] {$c$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
A student wrote $a^2+c^2=b^2$, where $c$ is the hypotenuse. Write the correct equation.
development.solution_text:
The hypotenuse $c$ must be squared on its own,
$a^2+b^2=c^2$

development.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (3,1);\coordinate (C) at (-1,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below right] {$e$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$f$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above right] {$d$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
This right-angled triangle is drawn on a slant. Write Pythagoras' theorem for it.

[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (4,0);\coordinate (B) at (0,0);\coordinate (C) at (4,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$d$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,right] {$e$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above left] {$f$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Write Pythagoras' theorem for this triangle.

mastery.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (4,0);\coordinate (C) at (0,3);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$p$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$r$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,above right] {$q$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Write Pythagoras' theorem for this triangle. Read the diagram carefully — the hypotenuse is not the last letter alphabetically.
mastery.solution_text:
The hypotenuse is $q$, so it is squared on its own,
$p^2+r^2=q^2$

mastery.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]\coordinate (A) at (0,0);\coordinate (B) at (2,-1);\coordinate (C) at (1,2);\draw (A)--(B) node[midway,below] {$u$};\draw (A)--(C) node[midway,left] {$v$};\draw (B)--(C) node[midway,right] {$w$};\coordinate (va) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(B)$);\coordinate (vb) at ($(A)!0.3cm!(C)$);\draw (va)--($(va)+(vb)-(A)$)--(vb);\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
This right-angled triangle is rotated so the right angle points down. Write Pythagoras' theorem for it.
A triangle has its hypotenuse labelled $a$ and its two shorter sides labelled $b$ and $c$. A student writes $a^2+b^2=c^2$. Explain the error and write the correct theorem.
mastery.solution_text:
The hypotenuse $a$ must be squared on its own, but the student put it with a shorter side. The correct theorem is
$b^2+c^2=a^2$
